meta.title: Dwarven Strongholds
meta.summary: Authored quest scenes for Gemloch and Felsrock, centered on mountain survival, citadel recovery, and dwarven salvage.

config.title: Gemloch Overseer Barak and the High Pass
config.idleText: The Range is always one bad step from killing someone. If I have nothing open for you, the mountain is either satisfied or gathering another lesson.

available.promptText: These peaks make fools out of the unbriefed. If you want to survive the Range, start by listening instead of climbing.
available.acceptLabel: Give me the mountain's first lesson.
available.acceptedText: Then hear it plain: Gemloch doesn't reward bravery. It rewards judgment under pressure and cold.
available.detailsLabel: What makes the Range different?
available.detailsText: Height, exposure, unstable passes, and ore-rich depths that make monsters as stubborn as the miners chasing them.
available.freeformText: Ask like you intend to remember it.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Barak what you should understand first about the Viridian Range.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What kills most people up here first?
available.freeformFallback: Barak narrows his eyes. "Ask one solid question and I'll answer it."

stages.completedText: Barak has given you his first mountain law. The work starts where the passes narrow.

available.promptText: Mountain Yetis have taken the lower passes and the caravans are paying for it in blood and broken wagons.
available.acceptedText: Four Mountain Yetis. Cull them before the lower passes become a graveyard with a trail through it.
available.detailsLabel: Why start there?
available.detailsText: Because supply lines decide whether a mountain settlement is a fortress or a starvation story.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Cold problems don't warm themselves while you hesitate.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Barak what you need before hunting the Mountain Yetis.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What kind of ground do they try to trap people on?
available.freeformFallback: Barak shifts his pickaxe. "Ask about the cull or take it."

active.promptText: The Yetis still hold the lower passes. Finish the cull.
active.resultText: Good. The caravans can breathe again, which means the mountain can afford to show you its next cruelty.
active.secondaryText: Four Mountain Yetis in the lower passes. Watch for the rush and don't let the terrain help them.
active.declineText: Cold doesn't wait. Neither do Yetis.

stages.completedText: The lower passes are quieter. Barak's next warning has scales.

available.promptText: Now the ridge wants to test your attention. Rock Basilisks make a religion out of one bad glance.
available.acceptedText: Four Rock Basilisks. Keep your eyes useful and your pride smaller than their stare.
available.detailsLabel: Why do they matter so much?
available.detailsText: Because the mountain doesn't only kill with force. Sometimes it kills by making you look exactly where you shouldn't.
available.freeformText: Ask. Just don't ask me how to survive if you plan to ignore the answer.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Barak what you need before facing the Rock Basilisks.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What mistake do they punish first?
available.freeformFallback: Barak grunts once. "Ask about the basilisks or get moving."

active.promptText: The basilisks are still nesting along the ridge. Finish the job.
active.resultText: Good. Now the ridge is readable again, which means we can chase ore instead of just surviving the climb.
active.secondaryText: Four Rock Basilisks along the ridge. Respect the angle of approach more than your instinct to rush.
active.declineText: Do that. Stone doesn't forgive impatience.

stages.completedText: The ridge is safer. Barak's next assignment is about greed and logistics.

available.promptText: The upper veins are rich with gold, but miners won't touch them without proof the route is worth the risk. Bring me ore and prove the mountain still pays.
available.acceptLabel: I'll bring back the gold ore.
available.acceptedText: Five Gold Ore. If you're going to risk the climb, make sure the mountain regrets giving anything up.
available.detailsLabel: Why is the ore important now?
available.detailsText: Because survival buys time, but resources buy permanence. Camps become settlements only when the mountain starts paying back what it costs.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Miners die faster when they romanticize a vein.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Barak what you need before the ore expedition.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What's the real risk on the upper veins?
available.freeformFallback: Barak scratches frost from his beard. "Ask sharp or take the haul."

active.promptText: The ore run isn't done. Bring back the gold and then we'll talk about keeping the heights open.
active.resultText: Good. Now we can justify holding the line up here. Which is useful, because the sky has started hunting the miners too.
active.secondaryText: Five Gold Ore from the upper veins. Take the route that keeps your footing first and greed second.
active.declineText: The vein won't move. The weather will.

stages.completedText: The ore expedition paid off. Barak's next threat comes from above.

available.promptText: Storm Condors have decided the cliff routes belong to them. Miners are getting lifted off the rock before they even draw breath to scream.
available.acceptedText: Three Storm Condors. Make the cliffs survivable again.
available.detailsLabel: Why are the condors so disruptive?
available.detailsText: Because vertical danger changes every calculation. One thing hunting from above can close a whole route without ever touching the ground.
available.freeformText: Ask. Air threats punish the lazy thinker first.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Barak what you need before facing the Storm Condors.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What makes the cliff routes so bad with condors on them?
available.freeformFallback: Barak folds his arms. "Ask about the condors or take the work."

active.promptText: The condors are still circling the cliffs. Finish the work.
active.resultText: Good. The upper routes can breathe again. That means the deeper tunnels are next.
active.secondaryText: Three Storm Condors. Stay off predictable ground where the dive can own you.

stages.completedText: The cliff routes are clearer. Barak's next problem is made from the mountain itself.

available.promptText: Gemloch Golems have started walking the deep tunnels again. Animated ore is bad enough. Animated ore in a mine is a message.
available.acceptedText: Three Gemloch Golems. Break them apart before the deep decides to close around us.
available.detailsLabel: Why call them a message?
available.detailsText: Because the mountain only animates its own body when something deeper has gone wrong. These are symptoms with fists.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Just don't mistake ore with a heartbeat for normal cave trouble.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Barak what you need before smashing the Gemloch Golems.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What are they really guarding down there?
available.freeformFallback: Barak's expression hardens. "Ask clearly or take the hammer to them."

active.promptText: The golems are still in the tunnels. Finish it and then we'll talk about what kind of mountain is waking up.
active.resultText: Good. Now the deeper threat can be named without sounding like superstition.
active.secondaryText: Three Gemloch Golems in the deep tunnels. Keep mobile and don't let a dead end become a coffin.
active.declineText: Later is how tunnels become tombs.

stages.completedText: The golems are down. Barak is ready to point you at the mountain's true titan.

available.promptText: The Avalanche Titan is not a monster in the usual sense. It's the mountain deciding motion should kill on purpose. Bring it down, or this whole range becomes negotiation with rockslides.
available.acceptedText: One Avalanche Titan. This is the kind of fight mountains remember.
available.detailsLabel: Why is the titan different?
available.detailsText: Because scale changes morale. Once a camp believes the mountain itself hates them, the pass is lost even before the first collapse.
available.freeformText: Ask. Final mountain fights deserve that much respect.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Barak what you need before facing the Avalanche Titan.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What kind of mistake gets people buried against it?
available.freeformFallback: Barak taps the stone with his boot. "Ask sharper or go make history."

active.promptText: The Avalanche Titan still walks. End it and then the summit can finally be tested cleanly.
active.resultText: Good. The mountain just got smaller in the right way. One last pass remains.
active.secondaryText: The Avalanche Titan. Respect momentum, falling terrain, and every instinct telling you to overcommit.

stages.completedText: The titan is down. Barak's last title waits at the summit.

available.promptText: One trial remains. Frost Giants descend when the blizzards hit, and if the high pass stays closed, the whole range eventually starves. Clear it and the summit is yours.
available.acceptedText: Six Frost Giants. Take the pass back from winter itself.
available.detailsLabel: What does the final title prove?
available.detailsText: That you can hold a mountain route open against everything the height, cold, and creatures here can throw at it.
available.freeformText: Then do it. Final questions should sound like readiness, not altitude sickness.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Barak how you're approaching the high-pass trial.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: I'm ready for the summit. What matters most?
available.freeformFallback: Barak gives a hard nod. "Take the pass or ask one last clean question."

active.promptText: The high pass is still contested. Finish the Frost Giants and come back.
active.resultText: Then hear it plain: the Range has tested your footing, your lungs, your greed, and your nerve. You still stand. That's enough for me.
active.secondaryText: That you can hold the high pass against winter, giants, and the mountain's own contempt.
active.declineText: Summits won later are usually won by someone else.

stages.completedText: Barak has already named you against the Range. The mountain keeps quieter company with you now.

config.title: Forgeguard Captain Haldor and the Broken Citadel
config.idleText: The Citadel still burns, just not always for us. If no order is open with me, then the next fight is deeper inside.

available.promptText: Felsrock isn't dead, but it's close enough to smell like failure. If you're here to help reclaim it, start by hearing what still stands and what turned against us.
available.acceptedText: Then listen. The Citadel still has structure, steel, and stubbornness. It lacks breathing room.
available.detailsLabel: What broke first?
available.detailsText: Control. The moment our own creations stopped recognizing command, every corridor became a question of whether the walls were still ours.
available.freeformText: Ask. Citadels fall slower than people think and faster than they deserve.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Haldor what you should understand first about reclaiming Felsrock.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What kind of fight is this really?
available.freeformFallback: Haldor's stare stays level. "Ask one useful thing."

stages.completedText: Haldor has given you the state of the Citadel. Now he needs metal answered with force.

available.promptText: Iron Automatons still patrol the halls like the command to stand down was never forged. Disable them and reclaim the corridors one section at a time.
available.acceptedText: Four Iron Automatons. Strip the hallway of its false guardians.
available.detailsLabel: Why start with the automatons?
available.detailsText: Because a fortress cannot recover while its own defenses are the first thing killing anyone who enters.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Steel problems deserve steel thinking.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Haldor what you need before clearing the Iron Automatons.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What should I expect from the corridor patrols?
available.freeformFallback: Haldor clenches his jaw. "Ask about the sweep or take it."

active.promptText: The automatons still control too much of the corridor. Finish the sweep.
active.resultText: Good. Now we can walk our own halls without asking permission from dead metal.
active.secondaryText: Four Iron Automatons. Keep the fight moving and don't let them pin you in narrow metal lanes.
active.declineText: Later gives machines more ground than they deserve.

stages.completedText: The hallways are cleaner. Haldor's next threat came from the furnace itself.

available.promptText: Molten Forgebound clawed out of the great furnace when control failed. Living slag with a memory for heat and destruction.
available.acceptedText: Four Molten Forgebound. Cool the forge floor by force if you have to.
available.detailsLabel: Why are they worse than automatons?
available.detailsText: Because machines can still pretend to be disciplined. Furnace-born things only remember appetite.
available.freeformText: Ask. Just don't ask me how to make lava polite.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Haldor what you need before facing the Molten Forgebound.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What makes the forge floor so dangerous now?
available.freeformFallback: Haldor gives a humorless grunt. "Ask clearly or start swinging."

active.promptText: The forge floor is still hostile. Finish the Forgebound and come back.
active.resultText: Good. The furnace stops feeling like a mouth for a moment. That gives us room to rebuild instead of just survive.
active.secondaryText: Four Molten Forgebound. Don't let heat and choke points become the real enemy.
active.declineText: Return when the forge floor is ours again.

stages.completedText: The forge floor is under better control. Haldor's next order is about rebuilding, not only killing.

available.promptText: A fortress isn't reclaimed by victory alone. We need material, structure, and proof the lower halls still serve more than memory. Craft the steel we need from salvage.
available.acceptedText: Three Steel Alloys. Bring the Citadel something it can build on.
available.detailsLabel: Why make time for salvage now?
available.detailsText: Because empty triumph collapses. Recovery has to put weight back into the walls.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Rebuilding is the harder discipline.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Haldor what you need before handling the deep salvage work.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What are we trying to restore first?
available.freeformFallback: Haldor's answer is blunt. "Ask about the salvage or do it."

active.promptText: The salvage work isn't done. Bring back the Steel Alloys and then we'll talk about what else wants the halls.
active.resultText: Good. That buys the Citadel structure. Unfortunately, the deep has started sending teeth of its own.
active.secondaryText: Craft three Steel Alloys from salvage in the lower halls.
active.declineText: Walls don't hold themselves together on promises.

stages.completedText: The salvage work is done. Haldor's next problem tunneled in from below.

available.promptText: Deep Dwellers have started tearing through the lower sections. If they keep undermining the east wing, we lose more than masonry.
available.acceptedText: Three Deep Dwellers. Stop them before the Citadel remembers how to collapse.
available.detailsLabel: Why are they so dangerous here?
available.detailsText: Because tunnel things attack more than bodies. They attack confidence in the floor itself.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Foundation fights deserve that much seriousness.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Haldor what you need before facing the Deep Dwellers.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What do they threaten besides the walls?
available.freeformFallback: Haldor's expression hardens. "Ask clearly or take the depths."

active.promptText: The Dwellers are still working the foundations. Finish it.
active.resultText: Good. The floor can be trusted for one more day. Now we deal with the runes that kept too much memory in them.
active.secondaryText: Three Deep Dwellers in the undermined sections. Don't let the terrain collapse the fight around you.

stages.completedText: The foundation threat is reduced. Haldor's next order glows in old glyphwork.

available.promptText: Rune Golems were never meant to wake without masters to command them. Now the old inscriptions are driving them in circles through the lower wards.
available.acceptedText: Three Rune Golems. Shatter the core runes and don't get sentimental about old craftsmanship.
available.detailsLabel: Why do the runes matter so much?
available.detailsText: Because when old protections lose context, they keep performing duty as ruin instead of order.
available.freeformText: Ask. Ancient mistakes deserve modern clarity.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Haldor what you need before facing the Rune Golems.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What turns their wards into a real threat?
available.freeformFallback: Haldor's answer is immediate. "Ask about the runes or break them."

active.promptText: The Rune Golems are still active. Break them and return.
active.resultText: Good. The old wards just lost their fists. Only the forge's will remains now.
active.secondaryText: Three Rune Golems. Break the core rune or you'll just keep fighting the same mistake.

stages.completedText: The runed guardians are broken. Haldor is ready to march you toward the master of this failure.

available.promptText: The Forgemaster Infernal has been sitting at the center of this catastrophe, driving every construct and furnace-born thing through the Citadel. End it and we take Felsrock back in spirit if not yet in full.
available.acceptedText: One Forgemaster Infernal. Make the great forge ours again.
available.detailsLabel: Why is this the true center?
available.detailsText: Because systems only become disasters at this scale when one controlling will turns every tool into a weapon at once.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Final citadel fights deserve discipline.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Haldor what you need before facing the Forgemaster Infernal.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What makes this fight different from the rest of the reclamation?
available.freeformFallback: Haldor grips his blade. "Ask sharp or go kill the thing."

active.promptText: The Forgemaster still controls too much of the Citadel. End it.
active.resultText: Good. The core of the disaster just broke. One last reckoning remains in the throne room.
active.secondaryText: The Forgemaster Infernal. This is the heart of the Citadel's corruption, not another strong machine.

stages.completedText: The Forgemaster has fallen. Haldor's last order belongs to the old throne.

available.promptText: One last mission. Corrupted Dwarf Kings still haunt the throne room like greed itself learned how to wear a crown after death. Put them down and close this chapter properly.
available.acceptedText: Five Corrupted Dwarf Kings. Lay the throne room to rest.
available.detailsLabel: Why end in the throne room?
available.detailsText: Because every reclaimed fortress has to decide whether it still respects the ghosts that built it. This is where respect ends and judgment begins.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Final reckonings should sound like resolve.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Haldor how you're approaching the throne-room reckoning.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What should I understand before I face the kings?
available.freeformFallback: Haldor's tone is low. "Take the reckoning or ask a worthy final question."

active.promptText: The throne room is not settled yet. Finish the reckoning and come back.
active.resultText: Then hear it from me: Felsrock is still wounded, but it is no longer bowing to what broke it. You helped make that true.
active.secondaryText: That the Citadel's final authority belongs to the living who reclaim it, not the dead who refuse to leave it.
active.declineText: Thrones left for later rarely get kinder.

stages.completedText: Haldor has already given his judgment on what you did for Felsrock.

config.title: Runesmith Korra and the Reforging Effort
config.idleText: The forge can cool for an hour, not a year. If I've no work for you, then either we're ahead of disaster or disaster is winding up again.

available.promptText: The master forge went dark and half the Citadel forgot what it meant to build instead of burn. If you're willing to help restart proper craft, sit down and listen.
available.acceptLabel: Tell me what the forge needs.
available.acceptedText: Then hear this: rebuilding starts long before the furnace lights. It starts with someone refusing to accept ruin as the final shape.
available.detailsLabel: Why does your forge matter so much?
available.detailsText: Because once a stronghold stops making its own future, it begins living off inventory and memory. Both run out.
available.freeformText: Ask. If you're going to help a forge breathe again, I'd prefer you think while doing it.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Korra what you should understand first about restarting the Citadel's forge.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What really failed when the master forge went dark?
available.freeformFallback: Korra flicks ash from a blueprint. "Ask one useful question."

stages.completedText: Korra has outlined the rebuild. Now she needs raw material and working space.

available.promptText: Mithril Ore is sealed behind collapsed armory walls. If we want rune-forging back, we start by recovering what the Citadel still hides from itself.
available.acceptedText: Four Mithril Ore. Bring me enough to prove the rebuild is more than talk.
available.detailsLabel: Why Mithril first?
available.detailsText: Because master-grade work needs master-grade material. You don't restart a great forge by pretending scrap and excellence are the same thing.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Salvage is still a craft when you do it properly.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Korra what you need before recovering the Mithril Ore.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What matters most when I go after the sealed ore?
available.freeformFallback: Korra doesn't look up. "Ask about the haul or go get it."

active.promptText: The Mithril run isn't done. Bring back the ore and then we can pretend the forge has a future again.
active.resultText: Good. That gives us material. What we still don't have is a forge floor quiet enough to use it.
active.secondaryText: Four Mithril Ore from the sealed armory side of the Citadel.
active.declineText: Mithril doesn't walk off, but opportunity does.

stages.completedText: Korra has the ore she needs. Her next problem is the automaton mess around the forge.

available.promptText: Iron Automatons are still trampling the forge floor. I can't relight anything while metal with no judgment keeps smashing my equipment.
available.acceptedText: Three Iron Automatons. Make the workspace worthy of a furnace again.
available.detailsLabel: Why is this separate from Haldor's war?
available.detailsText: Because soldiers reclaim territory. Smiths reclaim function. I care less about winning a hall than winning a working forge.
available.freeformText: Ask. Precision matters more near anvils than bravado ever will.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Korra what you need before clearing the forge floor.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What part of the floor matters most to protect?
available.freeformFallback: Korra taps the anvil once. "Ask clearly or start breaking machines."

active.promptText: The forge floor still isn't clean enough to trust. Finish the clear-out.
active.resultText: Good. Now the real test begins. Materials and space mean nothing if you can't turn them into work.
active.secondaryText: Three Iron Automatons on the forge floor. Protect the workspace while you're dismantling them.
active.declineText: The forge cools every minute you delay.

stages.completedText: The workspace is usable. Korra's final demand is proof you can craft at Citadel standard.

available.promptText: Now prove you can do more than gather and clear. Craft the Reinforced Plates and show me the forge can shape skill again instead of only memory.
available.acceptedText: Two Reinforced Plates. If you can do master-grade work here, the Citadel still has a future worth fighting for.
available.detailsLabel: What does this final craft prove?
available.detailsText: That recovery is real when production restarts. A lit forge without capable hands is just a warmer ruin.
available.freeformText: Then ask. Final craft tests should sound like concentration, not fear.
available.freeformPrompt: Tell Korra what you need before the final reforge.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What separates usable work from Citadel-grade work here?
available.freeformFallback: Korra smiles thinly. "Ask a smith's question or get to work."

active.promptText: The reforge isn't complete. Bring me the Reinforced Plates and then we'll decide what this place still deserves.
active.resultText: Good. That's not nostalgia. That's production. The Citadel can still make something worthy of itself.
active.secondaryText: Craft two Reinforced Plates to prove the forge and the smith can both still operate at Felsrock standard.
active.declineText: Master-grade work does not improve by waiting for courage.

stages.completedText: Korra has already accepted your work as real reforging. The forge remembers.
